summary: Tests are well-structured behaviorally but have significant issues: the multi-select test doesn't actually assert multi-select behavior, the framework mismatch (node:test vs vitest metadata), and the interactive selection test's custom renderer immediately calls done() without simulating a real selection, making the assertions weak.

findings.title: Multi-select test does not assert that multiple worktrees are cleaned up
findings.details: The test titled 'worktree-cleanup interactive mode allows selecting multiple worktrees and cleans all of them' only asserts that `git worktree list` was called. It does not simulate the user selecting multiple worktrees (the renderer's done callback is called but never with a meaningful multi-select result), and it never asserts that multiple `git worktree remove` commands were executed. The test name promises verification of multi-cleanup but the assertions don't deliver.
findings.fix: Have the custom renderer's done() callback resolve with an array of selected worktrees (e.g. ['feat-a', 'feat-b']). Then assert that ctx.execCalls contains separate 'git worktree remove' calls for each selected worktree.
findings.requirement: worktree-cleanup interactive mode allows selecting multiple worktrees
evidence: assert.ok(listCall, 'should list worktrees to present multi-select options');

findings.title: Interactive selection test's renderer calls done() immediately without a selection value
findings.details: In the 'without args lists available worktrees' test, the custom renderer calls `done(result)` but `result` is never defined — it's the parameter of the renderer function which receives `tui, theme, keybindings, done`. The renderer is invoked but immediately calls done with undefined, so the test cannot verify that a selected worktree actually gets cleaned up after selection. There's no assertion that a `git worktree remove` command was issued after the selection.
findings.fix: Call done() with a meaningful selected value (e.g. 'feat-a' or ['feat-a']), and add an assertion that a corresponding `git worktree remove` exec call was made after the UI resolved.
findings.requirement: worktree-cleanup without args lists available worktrees via git and presents interactive selection
evidence: renderer({ stop: () => {}, start: () => {}, requestRender: () => {} }, {}, {}, (result: T) => resolve(result));

findings.title: Framework mismatch: metadata says vitest but tests use node:test
findings.details: Generator metadata specifies framework 'vitest' and outputPath under __tests__/extensions/, but the actual test code uses `import test from 'node:test'` and `import assert from 'node:assert/strict'`. While node:test may work in this repo, the metadata inconsistency could cause CI configuration issues if the test runner is set up for vitest.
findings.fix: Either change the imports to use vitest (`import { test, expect, describe } from 'vitest'`) to match the metadata, or update the metadata to reflect node:test. Check existing test files in __tests__/extensions/ to confirm which runner is actually used and match it.

findings.title: Command name lookup is overly permissive and may mask real failures
findings.details: Every test looks up the command as `ext.commands?.['worktree-cleanup'] ?? ext.commands?.['worktreeCleanup'] ?? ext.commands?.['cleanup']`. This triple-fallback means the tests would pass with any of three different command names, potentially accepting an incorrect implementation. The plan specifically says `/worktree-cleanup`.
findings.fix: Check the existing extensions/worktree.ts source to determine the actual command naming convention, and use only that name. If unknown, use 'worktree-cleanup' as the primary and fail explicitly if not found rather than falling back silently.
findings.requirement: worktree extension fits repository structure conventions (name, commands shape)

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Review and update __tests__/extensions/currently-it-is-not-possible-for-me-to-work-easily-on-the-sa.plan.test.ts
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Review and update __tests__/extensions/tdd-plan.test.ts
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Review and update __tests__/extensions/plan-feature-spec.plan.test.ts
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Unconfirmed product changes beyond the requested feature
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.
findings.requirement: Unconfirmed product changes beyond the requested feature

strengths: Good use of the makeCtx pattern matching existing test conventions in the repo
Tests are genuinely behavioral — they exercise the command handler through a realistic context object rather than scanning source code
Good coverage of the backward-compatibility path (explicit slug argument)
Edge cases for no worktrees and git failure are well-conceived
Tests are designed to be RED-first, appropriate for TDD
